record.title: James Gleave, Interview - MP3 Recording
record.subject: Latah County -- Idaho
record.occupation: Mill worker at Potlatch Lumber Company
record.keywords: mills timber Great Depression immigrants Greek Americans Italian Americans lumberjacks IWW Prohibition saloons world wars Japanese Americans sports law enforcement logging

point.title: Introduction
Working in the mill; hard labor; Larry didn’t come very often to check up on the mill.
Wanted to put the Mill in Moscow originally, but there wasn’t enough water. Had lots of timber in Potlatch.
People had mixed feelings about Larry (?) Influenced where the Mill would be placed; if he had a job to do, he did it. Potlatch was always a dry town.
His father was constable, maintained the town. Also watched the Steam Line, maintenance and such. Started that around 1911 or 1913.
Potlatch Forestry Co. took over Potlatch Lumber.
Policing was a small part of his job.
Wages decreased; they kept him because he owed money on the land. Many men were put out of jobs from the Mill during the Depression; tried to keep foremen and sheriffs.
Told story about the garbage man on the night he quit; doesn’t remember everything about Potlatch, didn’t alleyways.

Never paid much attention to Potlatch, owned some land up there called Princeton. Converted a building in that area from a boarding house to a gym. Pool Hall, tried to keep people under 21 out of there.
Greeks and Italians stuck together in Potlatch.
Disagrees with Father about tips in restaurants; father believed accepting tips was an act of servitude.
IWW’s hated Potlatch; wanted better working conditions.
Potlatch room and board for lumberjacks; furnished very well; membership card stamped in tin.
Took courses at local college; took communication courses, mechanical courses.

Spent two years in the 6th grade and flunked out of school. Joined the IWW Wobblies. Prohibition; liquor was illegal in Idaho. Elks Lodge. Up until the Depression, railroads did not want you to ride the cars; with Depression, whole families traveled around that way.
Job of cleaning the jail.
Prohibition era. A prosecuting attorney went to the high-class bars during prohibition. Prohibition was a “farce”; easy to buy alcohol now. Had saloons in Grishman (?); maybe when he was a kid.

During WWI, some of them were at the box factory and someone accidentally sawed off four of their fingers; he picked up the fingers and put them in his pocket while they took the guy to the doctor to get them sewed on again.
Safety building inspection; many people didn’t have cars, they traveled by bikes, horses and such.
Lived in a Company owned town outside of Potlatch; a mining town. Men were cheap; if they lost a man during work accident, it was easy to replace.
Many Japanese immigrants; fifty percent of immigrants in Potlatch was Swedish.
No liquor in the camps or the woods; lumberjacks went to town to go to bars, especially single men; more men than women
People with the best jobs lived on Nob Hill, also known as Snob Hill.
Liked to play cards, but was “a born loser”; he worked too hard for his money to lose it while gambling.
Baseball teams in Potlatch. Numerous teams in the area

record.description: Working at mill; the town of Potlatch; wages and working conditions; immigrant workers; education; Prohibition era and the Great Depression; baseball in Potlatch.
record.rights: Digital format copyright: University of Idaho Library. Original Copyright: Latah County Historical Society
